note: Amina is a 9-year-old girl who came into care last Thursday following a welfare check. She was born in Somalia and speaks Somali as her first language — her English is functional for school but she becomes withdrawn and anxious when she can't communicate comfortably, especially with adults she doesn't know yet. She follows a Halal diet and this is non-negotiable for her family and her own sense of normalcy right now. Amina has been diagnosed with generalized anxiety and her outpatient therapist has strongly recommended weekly sessions continue without interruption — she's made real progress and a gap in care at this point would be setback. She is currently enrolled at Jefferson Elementary in the SE district and her teacher reports she is at grade level and has a small group of friends there, so keeping her in that school would be a significant stabilizing factor if at all possible. No siblings in the system. No known medical conditions beyond the anxiety diagnosis. She likes drawing and is reportedly very gentle and quiet. She does not present behavioral challenges.

Marcus is a 14-year-old male with a significant placement history — this is his fourth entry into care. He was diagnosed with PTSD following prolonged exposure to domestic violence in the home. Marcus has a behavioral file: two prior placements ended due to property destruction during dysregulation episodes, and one ended after a physical altercation with another foster child. That said, his last placement supervisor noted that when Marcus felt safe and respected, he was thoughtful, funny, and showed real leadership qualities with younger kids. He needs a trauma-informed home — this is not optional, it's clinical. Families without that training have struggled and the disruptions have compounded his distrust of adults. He does not have dietary restrictions. District doesn't matter much as he's already behind a grade and we're more focused on stability than school continuity at this point. He's English-only. No siblings in the system. Crowded homes have historically been a trigger — he does better with fewer kids around and a predictable daily structure. He mentioned wanting to learn to drive someday. Small things matter with Marcus.

Sofia and Lucia are 6-year-old twins who must be placed together — this is a hard requirement and has been documented at the case level. Separating them is not on the table. Both girls are Spanish-dominant; their English is very limited and they're currently in an ESL program at their school. A Spanish-speaking household is strongly preferred — Lucia especially shuts down in environments where she can't communicate, and Sofia tends to become the de facto translator for her sister which puts an unfair burden on her. Speaking of Sofia: she has a diagnosed asthma condition, moderate severity, managed with a daily inhaler and an emergency rescue inhaler she carries. Pets in the home are a significant concern — animal dander has previously triggered her asthma episodes. This is a medical consideration that needs to be treated as a hard constraint, not a preference. No dietary restrictions for either child. The girls are sweet, imaginative, and very close — they have their own little world and placement workers have noted that as long as they have each other, they adapt reasonably well to new environments. ESL support at whatever school they transfer to would need to be confirmed before placement is finalized.
